model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the production seasons of the planning horizon, in chronological order; the first member is the opening season and each later member directly follows the one before it

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the work shifts that can be run within a season

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the ordered breakpoints of a piecewise-linear production curve, numbered from the lowest level upward; consecutive breakpoints bracket the operating segments of the curve

sets.name: prp
sets.doc: the two production-relationship rows: the labor row gives employees required at each breakpoint and the motor row gives motors produced at each breakpoint

sets.name: scp
sets.doc: the two shift-cost rows: the fixed row gives the cost of opening a shift and the labor row gives the per-employee cost of staffing a shift

params.name: d
params.doc: the demand for motors in each season, in motors; zero in seasons with no demand

params.name: lc
params.doc: the leasing cost charged in each season when the lease-rent option is taken, in dollars per season; zero in seasons with no leasing charge

params.name: ei
params.doc: an externally given change in employment that occurs at the start of each season, in employees; zero in seasons with no external change

params.name: mc
params.doc: the material cost incurred per motor produced, in dollars per motor

params.name: sr
params.doc: the space-rental rate charged per motor of inventory held, in dollars per motor

params.name: hc
params.doc: the hiring cost incurred per employee hired, in dollars per employee

params.name: fc
params.doc: the firing cost incurred per employee fired, in dollars per employee

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to each season's costs to bring them to present value; equals 1 in the first season and shrinks geometrically in later seasons

params.name: invmax
params.doc: an upper bound on inventory, in motors, equal to the total demand summed over all seasons

params.name: pr
params.doc: the production-relationship table: pr['motor', l] is the number of motors produced at breakpoint l and pr['labor', l] is the number of employees required at breakpoint l

params.name: sc
params.doc: the shift-cost table: sc['fixed', s] is the fixed cost of opening shift s and sc['labor', s] is the cost per employee of staffing shift s, in dollars

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: isc
vars.doc: the inventory storage cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: wfc
vars.doc: the workforce fluctuation cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: src
vars.doc: the space-rental cost in each season, in dollars per season

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear production segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; these are the SOS2-type convex weights that place a shift's operating point along the production curve

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: the 0/1 selector variables that accompany the segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; used to enforce the SOS2 adjacency of the active segment weights

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the inventory of motors carried at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: the single lease-rent option indicator; equals 1 if the rental option is taken and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total employment in each season, in employees

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the employment on each shift of each season, in employees per shift

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: the shift-use indicator; equals 1 if the shift is opened in that season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired at the start of each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired at the start of each season

constraints.name: dpc[q]
constraints.intent: The direct production cost of each season is the cost of the motors made plus the cost of running the shifts. For each season, take the material cost of every motor produced, add the fixed cost of each shift that is opened and the staffing cost for the employees working that shift, and set the season's direct production cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: p[q]
constraints.intent: Production in each season is built up from the piecewise-linear segment weights chosen for the shifts. For each season, the number of motors produced equals the motor output associated with each breakpoint weighted by the corresponding segment weight, summed over all breakpoints and all shifts.

constraints.name: se[q,
constraints.intent: The employees staffed on a shift follow from the segment weights chosen for that shift. For each season and shift, the shift employment equals the labor requirement at each breakpoint weighted by that breakpoint's segment weight, summed over all breakpoints.

constraints.name: ss[l,
constraints.intent: The segment weights on a shift are active only when that shift is opened. For each season and shift, the segment weights summed over all breakpoints must equal the shift's open indicator, so the weights add up to one when the shift runs and to zero when it does not.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Inventory carries over from one season to the next as production meets demand. For each season, the ending inventory equals the inventory left from the immediately preceding season plus the motors produced that season minus the demand that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so nothing carries in.

constraints.name: isc[q]
constraints.intent: The inventory storage cost of each season combines the leasing charge and the space rental. For each season, take the leasing cost that applies when the lease option is taken, add the space-rental cost incurred that season, and set the season's inventory storage cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: src[q]
constraints.intent: The space-rental cost in each season pays for inventory held beyond what the lease option covers. For each season, the space-rental cost must be at least the rental rate applied to the inventory held that season after allowing for the inventory the lease option absorbs when it is taken.

constraints.name: wfc[q]
constraints.intent: The workforce fluctuation cost of each season pays for the hiring and firing done that season. For each season, take the hiring cost for every employee hired and the firing cost for every employee fired, and set the season's workforce fluctuation cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: e[q]
constraints.intent: Total employment in a season is the sum of the people working across its shifts. For each season, the total employment equals the shift employment summed over all shifts.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Employment evolves from one season to the next through hiring, firing and external changes. For each season, the total employment equals the employment carried from the immediately preceding season plus the people hired, minus the people fired, plus the external employment change for that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so none carries in.

constraints.name: ssb[l,
constraints.intent: Exactly one breakpoint selector is chosen on each shift. For each season and shift, the selectors summed over all breakpoints must equal one.

constraints.name: ss[l-1,
constraints.intent: The segment weights are tied to the chosen breakpoint selectors so that only weights adjacent to the selected breakpoint can be active. For the lowest breakpoint, that breakpoint's weight cannot exceed its own selector. For the second breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at those two breakpoints. For every higher breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at that breakpoint and the two just below it. This is applied for every breakpoint, season and shift.

constraints.name: pup
constraints.intent: Production in spring is capped at a fraction of the maximum the shifts could deliver. The motors produced in spring must not exceed eighty percent of the number of shifts times the largest motor output across all breakpoints.

query: We're looking at pulling the brakes on the front-loaded build — the combined output of the summer and fall quarters together held under 10000 motors so we're not sinking cash into early production. What does the optimizer return for the schedule and total cost under that ceiling?

model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep a second crew running through the cooler stretch — engineering wants at least one of fall or winter to light up its second shift so the line isn't bottlenecked on a single crew right before the spring rush. Plug that in and tell me what the schedule and total cost come out to.

query: There's a proposal to put a hard ceiling on how much stock we ever tie up — total motors in inventory summed across all four quarters held to 30000. Plug that in and walk me through the resulting schedule and total cost.
expected_pyomo: model.inv_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.inv[q] for q in model.q) <= 30000.0)

constraints.name: p[q]
constraints.intent: Production in each season is built up from the piecewise-linear segment weights chosen for the shifts. For each season, the number of motors produced equals the motor output associated with each breakpoint weighted by the corresponding segment weight, summed over all breakpoints and all shifts.

constraints.name: se[q,
constraints.intent: The employees staffed on a shift follow from the segment weights chosen for that shift. For each season and shift, the shift employment equals the labor requirement at each breakpoint weighted by that breakpoint's segment weight, summed over all breakpoints.

constraints.name: ss[l,
constraints.intent: The segment weights on a shift are active only when that shift is opened. For each season and shift, the segment weights summed over all breakpoints must equal the shift's open indicator, so the weights add up to one when the shift runs and to zero when it does not.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Inventory carries over from one season to the next as production meets demand. For each season, the ending inventory equals the inventory left from the immediately preceding season plus the motors produced that season minus the demand that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so nothing carries in.

constraints.name: isc[q]
constraints.intent: The inventory storage cost of each season combines the leasing charge and the space rental. For each season, take the leasing cost that applies when the lease option is taken, add the space-rental cost incurred that season, and set the season's inventory storage cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: src[q]
constraints.intent: The space-rental cost in each season pays for inventory held beyond what the lease option covers. For each season, the space-rental cost must be at least the rental rate applied to the inventory held that season after allowing for the inventory the lease option absorbs when it is taken.

constraints.name: wfc[q]
constraints.intent: The workforce fluctuation cost of each season pays for the hiring and firing done that season. For each season, take the hiring cost for every employee hired and the firing cost for every employee fired, and set the season's workforce fluctuation cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: e[q]
constraints.intent: Total employment in a season is the sum of the people working across its shifts. For each season, the total employment equals the shift employment summed over all shifts.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Employment evolves from one season to the next through hiring, firing and external changes. For each season, the total employment equals the employment carried from the immediately preceding season plus the people hired, minus the people fired, plus the external employment change for that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so none carries in.

constraints.name: ssb[l,
constraints.intent: Exactly one breakpoint selector is chosen on each shift. For each season and shift, the selectors summed over all breakpoints must equal one.

constraints.name: ss[l-1,
constraints.intent: The segment weights are tied to the chosen breakpoint selectors so that only weights adjacent to the selected breakpoint can be active. For the lowest breakpoint, that breakpoint's weight cannot exceed its own selector. For the second breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at those two breakpoints. For every higher breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at that breakpoint and the two just below it. This is applied for every breakpoint, season and shift.

constraints.name: pup
constraints.intent: Production in spring is capped at a fraction of the maximum the shifts could deliver. The motors produced in spring must not exceed eighty percent of the number of shifts times the largest motor output across all breakpoints.

query: HR is pitching a no-mass-layoffs guardrail across the whole year — no single quarter should let go more than six people, to keep severance and morale hits contained. With that per-quarter separation cap applied in every season, what does the optimizer return for the staffing plan and total cost?

model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the production seasons of the planning horizon, in chronological order; the first member is the opening season and each later member directly follows the one before it

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the work shifts that can be run within a season

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the ordered breakpoints of a piecewise-linear production curve, numbered from the lowest level upward; consecutive breakpoints bracket the operating segments of the curve

sets.name: prp
sets.doc: the two production-relationship rows: the labor row gives employees required at each breakpoint and the motor row gives motors produced at each breakpoint

sets.name: scp
sets.doc: the two shift-cost rows: the fixed row gives the cost of opening a shift and the labor row gives the per-employee cost of staffing a shift

params.name: d
params.doc: the demand for motors in each season, in motors; zero in seasons with no demand

params.name: lc
params.doc: the leasing cost charged in each season when the lease-rent option is taken, in dollars per season; zero in seasons with no leasing charge

params.name: ei
params.doc: an externally given change in employment that occurs at the start of each season, in employees; zero in seasons with no external change

params.name: mc
params.doc: the material cost incurred per motor produced, in dollars per motor

params.name: sr
params.doc: the space-rental rate charged per motor of inventory held, in dollars per motor

params.name: hc
params.doc: the hiring cost incurred per employee hired, in dollars per employee

params.name: fc
params.doc: the firing cost incurred per employee fired, in dollars per employee

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to each season's costs to bring them to present value; equals 1 in the first season and shrinks geometrically in later seasons

params.name: invmax
params.doc: an upper bound on inventory, in motors, equal to the total demand summed over all seasons

params.name: pr
params.doc: the production-relationship table: pr['motor', l] is the number of motors produced at breakpoint l and pr['labor', l] is the number of employees required at breakpoint l

params.name: sc
params.doc: the shift-cost table: sc['fixed', s] is the fixed cost of opening shift s and sc['labor', s] is the cost per employee of staffing shift s, in dollars

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: isc
vars.doc: the inventory storage cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: wfc
vars.doc: the workforce fluctuation cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: src
vars.doc: the space-rental cost in each season, in dollars per season

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear production segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; these are the SOS2-type convex weights that place a shift's operating point along the production curve

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: the 0/1 selector variables that accompany the segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; used to enforce the SOS2 adjacency of the active segment weights

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the inventory of motors carried at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: the single lease-rent option indicator; equals 1 if the rental option is taken and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total employment in each season, in employees

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the employment on each shift of each season, in employees per shift

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: the shift-use indicator; equals 1 if the shift is opened in that season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired at the start of each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired at the start of each season

constraints.name: dpc[q]
constraints.intent: The direct production cost of each season is the cost of the motors made plus the cost of running the shifts. For each season, take the material cost of every motor produced, add the fixed cost of each shift that is opened and the staffing cost for the employees working that shift, and set the season's direct production cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: p[q]
constraints.intent: Production in each season is built up from the piecewise-linear segment weights chosen for the shifts. For each season, the number of motors produced equals the motor output associated with each breakpoint weighted by the corresponding segment weight, summed over all breakpoints and all shifts.

constraints.name: se[q,
constraints.intent: The employees staffed on a shift follow from the segment weights chosen for that shift. For each season and shift, the shift employment equals the labor requirement at each breakpoint weighted by that breakpoint's segment weight, summed over all breakpoints.

constraints.name: ss[l,
constraints.intent: The segment weights on a shift are active only when that shift is opened. For each season and shift, the segment weights summed over all breakpoints must equal the shift's open indicator, so the weights add up to one when the shift runs and to zero when it does not.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Inventory carries over from one season to the next as production meets demand. For each season, the ending inventory equals the inventory left from the immediately preceding season plus the motors produced that season minus the demand that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so nothing carries in.

constraints.name: isc[q]
constraints.intent: The inventory storage cost of each season combines the leasing charge and the space rental. For each season, take the leasing cost that applies when the lease option is taken, add the space-rental cost incurred that season, and set the season's inventory storage cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: src[q]
constraints.intent: The space-rental cost in each season pays for inventory held beyond what the lease option covers. For each season, the space-rental cost must be at least the rental rate applied to the inventory held that season after allowing for the inventory the lease option absorbs when it is taken.

constraints.name: wfc[q]
constraints.intent: The workforce fluctuation cost of each season pays for the hiring and firing done that season. For each season, take the hiring cost for every employee hired and the firing cost for every employee fired, and set the season's workforce fluctuation cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: e[q]
constraints.intent: Total employment in a season is the sum of the people working across its shifts. For each season, the total employment equals the shift employment summed over all shifts.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Employment evolves from one season to the next through hiring, firing and external changes. For each season, the total employment equals the employment carried from the immediately preceding season plus the people hired, minus the people fired, plus the external employment change for that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so none carries in.

constraints.name: ssb[l,
constraints.intent: Exactly one breakpoint selector is chosen on each shift. For each season and shift, the selectors summed over all breakpoints must equal one.

constraints.name: ss[l-1,
constraints.intent: The segment weights are tied to the chosen breakpoint selectors so that only weights adjacent to the selected breakpoint can be active. For the lowest breakpoint, that breakpoint's weight cannot exceed its own selector. For the second breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at those two breakpoints. For every higher breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at that breakpoint and the two just below it. This is applied for every breakpoint, season and shift.

constraints.name: pup
constraints.intent: Production in spring is capped at a fraction of the maximum the shifts could deliver. The motors produced in spring must not exceed eighty percent of the number of shifts times the largest motor output across all breakpoints.

query: Summer carries the heaviest build on the whole calendar while the other three quarters coast. Shouldn't summer's output stay under about a fifth of the year's total production instead of front-loading the line that hard — say no more than 22% of all four quarters combined? What's the model seeing that justifies leaning on summer that much?
expected_pyomo: model.summer_share = Constraint(expr=model.p['summer'] <= 0.22 * sum(model.p[q] for q in model.q))

query: The plan churns through a lot of people over the year — separations stack up across the quarters. Shouldn't total firings for the whole year stay under eight so we're not constantly cutting and backfilling crew? What's pulling the model toward that much turnover?
expected_pyomo: model.total_firings = Constraint(expr=sum(model.f[q] for q in model.q) <= 8.0)

query: Stock balloons toward the back half — we're parking a mountain of motors in the warehouse going into winter. Shouldn't end-of-quarter inventory be held under 15000 motors in every season so we're not tying up that much cash in a warehouse at any point? What's keeping the model from spreading the build out instead?

sets.doc: the ordered breakpoints of a piecewise-linear production curve, numbered from the lowest level upward; consecutive breakpoints bracket the operating segments of the curve

sets.name: prp
sets.doc: the two production-relationship rows: the labor row gives employees required at each breakpoint and the motor row gives motors produced at each breakpoint

sets.name: scp
sets.doc: the two shift-cost rows: the fixed row gives the cost of opening a shift and the labor row gives the per-employee cost of staffing a shift

params.name: d
params.doc: the demand for motors in each season, in motors; zero in seasons with no demand

params.name: lc
params.doc: the leasing cost charged in each season when the lease-rent option is taken, in dollars per season; zero in seasons with no leasing charge

params.name: ei
params.doc: an externally given change in employment that occurs at the start of each season, in employees; zero in seasons with no external change

params.name: mc
params.doc: the material cost incurred per motor produced, in dollars per motor

params.name: sr
params.doc: the space-rental rate charged per motor of inventory held, in dollars per motor

params.name: hc
params.doc: the hiring cost incurred per employee hired, in dollars per employee

params.name: fc
params.doc: the firing cost incurred per employee fired, in dollars per employee

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to each season's costs to bring them to present value; equals 1 in the first season and shrinks geometrically in later seasons

params.name: invmax
params.doc: an upper bound on inventory, in motors, equal to the total demand summed over all seasons

params.name: pr
params.doc: the production-relationship table: pr['motor', l] is the number of motors produced at breakpoint l and pr['labor', l] is the number of employees required at breakpoint l

params.name: sc
params.doc: the shift-cost table: sc['fixed', s] is the fixed cost of opening shift s and sc['labor', s] is the cost per employee of staffing shift s, in dollars

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: isc
vars.doc: the inventory storage cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: wfc
vars.doc: the workforce fluctuation cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: src
vars.doc: the space-rental cost in each season, in dollars per season

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear production segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; these are the SOS2-type convex weights that place a shift's operating point along the production curve

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: the 0/1 selector variables that accompany the segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; used to enforce the SOS2 adjacency of the active segment weights

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the inventory of motors carried at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: the single lease-rent option indicator; equals 1 if the rental option is taken and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total employment in each season, in employees

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the employment on each shift of each season, in employees per shift

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: the shift-use indicator; equals 1 if the shift is opened in that season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired at the start of each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired at the start of each season

constraints.name: dpc[q]
constraints.intent: The direct production cost of each season is the cost of the motors made plus the cost of running the shifts. For each season, take the material cost of every motor produced, add the fixed cost of each shift that is opened and the staffing cost for the employees working that shift, and set the season's direct production cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: p[q]
constraints.intent: Production in each season is built up from the piecewise-linear segment weights chosen for the shifts. For each season, the number of motors produced equals the motor output associated with each breakpoint weighted by the corresponding segment weight, summed over all breakpoints and all shifts.

constraints.name: se[q,
constraints.intent: The employees staffed on a shift follow from the segment weights chosen for that shift. For each season and shift, the shift employment equals the labor requirement at each breakpoint weighted by that breakpoint's segment weight, summed over all breakpoints.

constraints.name: ss[l,
constraints.intent: The segment weights on a shift are active only when that shift is opened. For each season and shift, the segment weights summed over all breakpoints must equal the shift's open indicator, so the weights add up to one when the shift runs and to zero when it does not.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Inventory carries over from one season to the next as production meets demand. For each season, the ending inventory equals the inventory left from the immediately preceding season plus the motors produced that season minus the demand that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so nothing carries in.

constraints.name: isc[q]
constraints.intent: The inventory storage cost of each season combines the leasing charge and the space rental. For each season, take the leasing cost that applies when the lease option is taken, add the space-rental cost incurred that season, and set the season's inventory storage cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: src[q]
constraints.intent: The space-rental cost in each season pays for inventory held beyond what the lease option covers. For each season, the space-rental cost must be at least the rental rate applied to the inventory held that season after allowing for the inventory the lease option absorbs when it is taken.

constraints.name: wfc[q]
constraints.intent: The workforce fluctuation cost of each season pays for the hiring and firing done that season. For each season, take the hiring cost for every employee hired and the firing cost for every employee fired, and set the season's workforce fluctuation cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: e[q]
constraints.intent: Total employment in a season is the sum of the people working across its shifts. For each season, the total employment equals the shift employment summed over all shifts.

constraints.name: q)
constraints.intent: Employment evolves from one season to the next through hiring, firing and external changes. For each season, the total employment equals the employment carried from the immediately preceding season plus the people hired, minus the people fired, plus the external employment change for that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so none carries in.

constraints.name: ssb[l,
constraints.intent: Exactly one breakpoint selector is chosen on each shift. For each season and shift, the selectors summed over all breakpoints must equal one.

constraints.name: ss[l-1,
constraints.intent: The segment weights are tied to the chosen breakpoint selectors so that only weights adjacent to the selected breakpoint can be active. For the lowest breakpoint, that breakpoint's weight cannot exceed its own selector. For the second breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at those two breakpoints. For every higher breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at that breakpoint and the two just below it. This is applied for every breakpoint, season and shift.

constraints.name: pup
constraints.intent: Production in spring is capped at a fraction of the maximum the shifts could deliver. The motors produced in spring must not exceed eighty percent of the number of shifts times the largest motor output across all breakpoints.

query: Finance is uneasy about two things at once — we're paying for the overflow warehouse lease and still front-loading the line hard. Couldn't we just drop the lease and keep the combined summer-plus-fall build under 10000 motors at the same time, and live within the space we own? What's the model seeing that makes both of those moves cost us?
